coredata.dc:title: Selective optosensing rutin and quercetin by novel hybrid organic–inorganic lead-free copper halide perovskite
coredata.dc:creator: The Huy, Bui
Thi Cam Huong, Le
Sharipov, Mirkomil
Bang Truong, Hai
Lee, Yong-Ill
coredata.dc:description: An optical sensor based on fluorescence quenching has been developed for detecting Quercetin and Rutin. The signal source for this sensor is a hybrid organic–inorganic lead-free copper halide perovskite material, OA3Cu2I4Cl (OACuH), synthesized from octadecylamine C18H39N (OA), CuI, and CuCl. When excited at a wavelength of 355 nm, the resulting material exhibits stability and emits bright light. Characterization of the synthesized material was conducted using various methods including as X-ray powder diffraction, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and fluorescent analysis. Addition of Quercetin and Rutin to OACuH resulted in a reduction in its fluorescence. The proposed sensor demonstrates excellent selectivity and a low limit of detection (70 nM for quercetin and 50 nM for rutin) within the range of 0.1–200 µM. The reliability of the proposed assay was validated by quantifying Rutin and Quercetin in fruit samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lead-free
Flavonoid
Quenching
Hybrid perovskite
Fluorescence
Fruit sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grand funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (NRF-2023R1A2C1004414).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Effect of the different compositions on the emission spectra of the samples; (b) Absorption spectra of the samples with different compositions; (c) XRD pattern and (d) FTIR spectra of OA3Cu2I4Cl sample.

(a) OACuH powder sample exhibits strong orange color under the irradiation of UV light; (b) Emission-excitation spectra of OACuH in the aqueous solution; (c) Stability of OACu sample in the aqueous solution (0.2 mg/mL) using PBS buffer with triplet repetitions at various pH values.
(a) Overlap between the absorption spectra of rutin and quercetin with the excitation spectrum of the OACuH sample; (b) Fluorescence decay curves of OACuH samples w/o the analytes.

Changes of the FL spectra of the OACuH by Que (a) and Rut (c) concentration. Relationship between Que (b) and Rut (d) concentration and I/I0 – where I and I0 are the FL intensities in the presence and absence of the analyte.

(a) FL intensity of OACuH sensor in the presence of Que (as a Ref. sample) and the mixture of Que and potential interfering species (alanine, aspartic acid, arginine, ascorbic acid, cysteine, glucose, glutamine, glutathione, glycine, histidine, lysine, proline, phenylalanine, tryptophan, tyrosine, and valine); (b) Ratio between the FL intensity of control sample, only Que, and a combination of Que and metal ions; (c) Influence of incubation on the fluorescence spectra of OACuH with the addition of BSA-combined Que and BSA-combined Rut; (d) Absorption spectra of BSA-combined Que and BSA-combined Rut after the mixture was /wasn’t incubated.
Recovery tests on the skin-peeled apple and green grapes samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * The 95 % confidence interval was calculated with Student’s t = 2.776 (n = 5).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection limit and linear range compared to other approaches.

head.ce:title: Selective optosensing rutin and quercetin by novel hybrid organic–inorganic lead-free copper halide perovskite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Applied Technology, School of Technology, Van Lang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Anastro Laboratory, Institute of Basic Science, Changwon National University, Changwon 51140, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optical sensor based on fluorescence quenching has been developed for detecting Quercetin and Rutin. The signal source for this sensor is a hybrid organic–inorganic lead-free copper halide perovskite material, OA3Cu2I4Cl (OACuH), synthesized from octadecylamine C18H39N (OA), CuI, and CuCl. When excited at a wavelength of 355 nm, the resulting material exhibits stability and emits bright light. Characterization of the synthesized material was conducted using various methods including as X-ray powder diffraction, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and fluorescent analysis. Addition of Quercetin and Rutin to OACuH resulted in a reduction in its fluorescence. The proposed sensor demonstrates excellent selectivity and a low limit of detection (70 nM for quercetin and 50 nM for rutin) within the range of 0.1–200 µM. The reliability of the proposed assay was validated by quantifying Rutin and Quercetin in fruit samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin, a type of polyphenolic flavonoid, is abundant in fruits, edible vegetables, and wine, and has piqued the interest of researchers due to its structure, which makes it function as an antioxidant, antibacterial, anticancer agent, and protein kinase inhibitor. Various techniques have been employed to identify quercetin, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [1–3], thin-layer chromatography [4], electrochemistry [5–7], and fluorescence [8–13]. Rutin, on the other hand, is a flavonoid complex composed of natural glycosides present in flowers, seeds, and leaves, and has properties such as being a scavenger of superoxide anions, hydroxyl, and peroxyl radicals, and anticancer and antioxidant properties [14,15]. Despite its importance, finding a simple and precise method for detecting quercetin (Que) and rutin (Rut) is still challenging. The fluorescence technique has emerged as an effective approach for identifying chemical substances due to its high sensitivity, fast response, on-site monitoring, available visualization, and ease of use [16,17]. So far, fluorescence materials used in this approach have typically consisted of semiconductor quantum dots [18–20], upconversion materials [21,22], carbon dots [23–25], and conjugated polymers [26,27].

Halide perovskite materials have become a subject of great interest due to their advantageous properties such as large absorption coefficients, long carrier diffusion lengths, and excellent photoelectric characteristics, leading to their use in solar cells and light-emitting diodes. Cesium lead halide perovskite materials have gained significant attention from research groups due to their exceptional properties, such as high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and variable band-gap [28–30]. However, their commercial potential is hindered by their low stability, especially with regard to aqueous resistance, which is a significant issue for lead-based halide perovskite materials. In fact, cesium lead halide perovskite materials are not commonly used as fluorophores for biochemical analysis. Only a few reports have addressed the use of cesium lead halide perovskites for detecting chemical compounds dissolved in an aqueous solution. For instance, Huang's group embedded CsPbBr3 perovskite in molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica (PRS-MIM) to detect 2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate, but the test was carried out in dichloromethane [31]. Another study utilized CsPbBr3 as a fluorophore and the molecularly imprinted polymer approach to detect phoxim in dichloromethane by utilizing the FL quenching effect of CsPbBr3 [32]. Wang's team synthesized CsPbBr3 using oleic acid, octadecene, and oleylamine as the solvent, and attached it to the boron nitride sheet to create a tetracycline sensor that employs the fluorescence quenching effect of CsPbBr3. However, the analysis was conducted in a dichloromethane solution [53]. Our group reported an optical sensor for detecting tetracycline in food samples based on the fluorescence quenching of perofluorooctyltriethyloxylsilane fluorocarbons assembled on the particle surface of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 [33].

To address this issue, various lead-free halide perovskite materials have been developed by substituting lead with tin [34,35], bismuth [36,37], silver [38],and indium [39,40]. Copper has shown promise as a potential lead substitute in halide perovskite materials [41,42]. Several research groups have synthesized copper-based samples with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), including Cs3Cu2I5 produced by Jun's group [42], tunable emission colloidal nanocrystals Cs3Cu2X5 (X = I, Br, Cl) synthesized from Cs-oleate and CuX precursors using a hot injection method by Li's team [41], and β-Cs3Cu2Cl5 prepared by Zhang et. al. [43] Zhang’s group [44] used a solvent evaporation crystallization process to make Cs3Cu2I5 with zero dimensions. Other groups have also developed copper-based samples with improved thermal and air stability, including one-dimensional inorganic halides CsCu2X3 produced by Roccanova's team [45]. Despite their relatively stable fluorescence in the presence of air and moisture, the emissions of these copper-based samples quickly decrease when they are dispersed in an aqueous solution.

A novel category of perovskite material known as hybrid inorganic–organic halide perovskite (HHP) is formed by ionic and secondary bonds, contrasting with covalent bonds. Until recently, HHPs have been exclusively synthesized with short carbon chains [46,47]. We have achieved a breakthrough by successfully synthesizing lead-free HHP using octadecylamine C18H39N (OA), CuI, and CuCl as precursors. Our approach combines grinding technique and sonication in water to produce OA3Cu2I4Cl (OACuH). The study discovered that the fluorescence (FL) intensity of OACuH solution decreases in the presence of Rut and Que, leading to the development of a selective assay for Rut and Que with a simple yet high selectivity and low detection limit procedure. The reliability of the assay was evaluated using green grape and apple samples.

Octadecylamine C18H39N (OA) (99 %), cuprous chloride (CuCl, 99 %), copper(I) iodide (CuI, 99 %), hydriodic acid (HI, 57 % in water), and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Korea.

A transparent solution was produced by dissolving 5 mmol of OA in 75 mL of ethanol (EtOH) at room temperature. The OA solution was stirred at 0 °C while 8.8 mmol of HI solution was added. The temperature of the mixture was maintained at 0 °C for 24 h before being evaporated at 40 °C under a vacuum atmosphere to get powder. OAI was obtained by drying the collected material at room temperature after multiple diethyl ether washes. Using hydrochloride acid, octadecylamine chloride (OACl) was produced in a similar procedure.

OAI, OACl, CuI, and CuCl with various fractions were well homogenized in a mortar for 30 min before being transferred to a plastic bottle with 10 mL of water. The blend was sonicated at various temperatures (5, 30, 60, and 90 °C) for 30 min using a Qsonica sonicator (Q500, power rating of 500 W, frequency of 20 kHz, USA), before being centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The powder was dried for 12 h at 50 °C in a vacuum oven.

XRD diffractometer (X’Pert Pro, PANalytical, Netherland) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å) was used to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. The diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained on a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Jasco V-670, Japan). FT-IR spectra were measured using FT-IR spectroscopy (FT/IR-6300 Jasco, Japan). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were acquired on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-6500 Jasco, Japan). The composition of the OACuH was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 XPS System, Thermo Fisher Scientific U.K.). The morphology of the samples was observed with low-voltage scanning electron microscopy (Merlin compact, Zeiss, Germany). The lifetime measurements were carried out at the Korea Basic Science Institute, Daegu Center, Korea. An excitation source was a single-mode pulsed diode laser with a wavelength of 375 nm, a pulse width of 30 ps, and an average power of 0.1 to 1 μW. A single-photon avalanche diode (PDM series, MPD) was used to acquire emissions from the sample. Emission photons were counted using time-correlated single-photon counting equipment from PicoQuant GmbH in Germany (TimeHarp-260 and PicoHarp-300). A spectrometer (MicroTime-200, Picoquant, Germany) was used to analyze time-resolved fluorescence and phosphorescence. Using Symphotime-64 software (Ver. 2.2), exponential fitting was done for the obtained fluorescence and phosphorescence decays A liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (UltiMate 3000, UHPLC, Thermo Scientific, USA) was employed to quantify the analyses in real sample.

Korean apples and green grapes were purchased from convenience stores in Changwon, Korea. The juices were extracted by crushing skinned apples and green grapes in a liquidizer, followed by filtration using a paper filter. The resulting juices were stored in a refrigerated environment at 6 °C.

For fluorescence measurements, 100 µL of the juice samples were added to the OACuH solution (0.2 mg·mL−1) after being spiked with various Rut or Que concentrations. The fluorescence spectra of the spiked samples were recorded using 355 nm wavelength excitation, and the experiments were repeated five times.

For LC-MS measurements, the juice sample was mixed in a 1: 2 (v/v) ratio with methanol (MeOH), shaken for 15 min, and then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and subjected to a freeze-drying process. Following this, 3 mL of water was added, and the mixture was fractioned using a solid-phase extraction method employing a Discovery® DSC-18 SPE tube (bed wt. 500 mg, volume 6 mL, Sigma, USA) [48,49].

Fig. 1 a shows the photoluminescence spectra of OA3Cu2Cl5-xIx samples synthesized from the various proportions of OAI, OACl, CuCl, and CuI under an excitation of 355 nm wavelength. Except for their FL intensity, the emission spectra of the samples are similar in shape. The FL intensity of the samples increased as OAI/OACl ratio increased. In comparison to the others, the FL intensity of the OA3Cu2Cl4I, which was synthesized from OACl, CuCl, and CuI, was the weakest. Fig. 1b shows the absorption spectra of the OA3Cu2Cl5-xIx samples. The samples’ edge bands were altered from 475 nm to 510 nm, and the bandgap was calculated using (αhν)2 = hν -Eg. The optical bandgaps were estimated to be between 2.61 eV and 2.43 eV [46]. For further experiments, the ratio of the precursors was chosen to be OAI:CuCl:CuI = 3:1:1 based on the FL results. Furthermore, we discovered that the FL intensity of the OACuH sample improved when the sample was treated at 60 °C. The increase could be attributed to the effective interaction of the precursors, which resulted in the particles’ crystallizing structure being perfected. Fig. 1c displays the X-ray powder diffraction pattern of the OACuH sample. In comparison with the precursor database, the result validated the creation of the new structure. FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) of the OACuH sample was analyzed as follows: the peaks at 2925 cm−1, 2852 cm−1, 1473 cm−1, and 722 cm−1 correspond to asymmetrical C─H stretching, symmetrical C─H stretching, asymmetrical C─H bending, and methylene rocking, respectively, demonstrating the presence of OA. The absence of ─NH3 + stretching vibration at 3010 cm−1 indicated the structural perovskite. The existence of vibration modes related to C─H and N─H stretching at 3230 cm−1 and 3140 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of OACuH further confirmed the perovskite structure [50].

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken to estimate the valance state and chemical composition of the OACuH sample (Fig. S1). Fig. S1a displays the X-ray photoelectron survey spectrum of the prepared sample. The high-resolution spectra of C, Ni, Cu, I, and Cl are shown in Fig. S1b-S1f. The peak of C is located at 284 eV corresponding to C1 s (Fig. S1b). The peaks of N are attributed to N1 s (Fig. S1c). Binding energies of 952.21 eV and 932.3 eV for Cu2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively, in the Cu 2p XPS spectrum confirmed the existence of Cu+ (Fig. S1d). The peaks at 619.2 eV, 198.1 eV, and 198.4 eV are ascribed to the I3d5/2 and Cl2p, respectively (Fig. S1e & S1f) [51]. The data shows that the ratio of OA:Cu:I:Cl is 3:2:3.6:0.8. This value is close to the compound’s prepared stoichiometry.

The powder samples emitted bright orange color when exposed to UV light, as displayed in Fig. 2 a. To enhance the dispersion of the OACuH powder sample in an aqueous solution, it was treated with CTAB under sonication. The emission-excitation spectra of the OACuH sample dispersed in an aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 2b. The emission peak was observed at a wavelength of 618 nm, and the excitation spectra revealed an intense peak at 330 nm with a monitor wavelength of 618 nm. The PLQY of the OACuH sample was determined to be 35 %, as measured by the Jasco FP-6500 spectrophotometer's integrating sphere.

The size distribution of the OACuH sample in the aqueous solution was measured using the Nanoparticle Tracking Analysis (NTA) technology. This method involves analyzing both the light scattering and Brownian motion of the particles. Tests conducted using the NanoSight NS300 (Malvern Panalytical) showed that the mean hydrodynamic diameter of the OACuH particles was approximately 222 nm (refer to Fig. S2). Additionally, SEM images of the OACuH powder sample are presented in Fig. S3, which demonstrates that the particles were largely uniform in size and shape.

The FL intensity of the OACuH powder sample insignificantly changed after 3 months when the OACuH powder sample was stored in a desiccator. The study also investigated the stability of the OACuH solution under different pH conditions (Fig. 2c). It was observed that the FL intensity of the solution decreased in acidic media after 180 min, while there was negligible change in the initial FL intensity at higher pH values, particularly at pH 7. The subsequent experiments were carried out using phosphate buffer saline 7.2 (PBS).

According to Fig. 3 a, Que and Rut have two distinct absorption peaks at 260 nm and 370 nm, respectively, which correspond to two rings and the π → π* transitions in the flavonoid structure [52]. The excitation spectrum of OACuH overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Que and Rut, suggesting the possible involvement of inner filter effect (IFE) in the fluorescence quenching mechanism for the OACuH- Que/Rut solution [53]. To confirm IFE, fluorescence decay curves of the OACuH and OACuH-target samples were measured, as shown in Fig. 3b. The lifetime of the OACuH emission at 616 nm was found to be 5.26 µs, and did not change in the presence of the targets (Rut and Que) were present. Therefore, the FL quenching effect of OACuH by Que/Rut is attributed to the IFE mechanism [53].

It was observed that the FL intensity of the OACuH solution (0.2 mg·mL−1) decreased with increasing the Que/Rut concentration (Fig. 4 a & 4c). The dependences of FL intensity of OACuH on concentrations of Que/ Rut can be expressed using the equations ln(I/I0) = -0.08––13.2[C] (for Que) and ln(I/I0) = -0.069––15.17[C] (for Rut), where [C] is a the analytes’ concentration in mM, I and I0 denote the FL intensities of OACuH with/ without Que/Rut, respectively (Fig. 4b & 4d). The limits of detection for Que and Rut were determined to be 70 and 50 nM, respectively.

The study assessed the effect of various potential interfering substances, including alanine, aspartic acid, arginine, ascorbic acid, cysteine, glucose, glutamine, glutathione, glycine, histidine, lysine, proline, phenylalanine, tryptophan, tyrosine, and valine. The results indicated that despite the fifty-fold higher concentrations of the interfering chemicals compared to the control compounds (Que/Rut 0.1 mM), their impact on the FL intensity of the OACuH sample was insignificant (Fig. 5 a). Additionally, metal ions, such as Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+, have no discernible impact (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the suggested method is not impacted by common biological sample constituents.

As previously mentioned, the FL intensity of the OACuH decreased with the different levels by the presence of Rut and Que. Previous research has shown that bovine serum albumin (BSA) has different abilities in binding to Que and Rut. Taking advantage of this difference, Yu's group used BSA to mask Que and prevent the quenching of the FL of silicon nanoparticles [54]. In this work, the same masking approach was used with minor modifications. Initially, Rut or Que was combined with BSA in the PBS and incubated overnight at room temperature. Following incubation, the mixture was added to the OACuH solution, and the fluorescence spectra were acquired. The results showed that the FL intensity of the OACuH solution was unaffected by the addition of BSA-combined Que. Despite the addition of BSA-combined Rut or unadulterated Rut, the variation of the FL intensity of OACuH remained unchanged (Fig. 5c). This could be attributed to the aggregation of BSA-combined Que resulting in the unchanged FL intensity of OACuH. The absorption peak of Que disappeared when BSA-combined Que was incubated, whereas the absorption spectra of BSA-combined Run remained unchanged without incubation, as shown in Fig. 5d. These results demonstrate that our suggested assay is capable of differentiating between Que and Rut samples.

To evaluate the accuracy of our proposed sensor, we performed recovery tests on Rut and Que using actual samples. The FL spectra of the spiked samples were recorded using 355 nm wavelength excitation, and the experiments were repeated five time. The results are summarized in Table 1 and found that the recovery levels ranged from 95.1 % to 103.7 % with RSD lower than 7.1 %, which indicates that the assay can effectively detect these compound in complex matrices. Que and Rut amounts were found to be 0.064 mg·100-1 g of apple and 0.106 mg·100-1 g of green grapes, respectively.

Additionally, the quantity of Que (in the apple sample) and Rut (in the green grape sample) was measured by the LC-MS method to verify the validity of our proposed sensor. Mass spectra of quercetin (from apple samples) and rutin (from green grapes sample) are shown in Fig. S4. Using this method, the amounts of Que and Rut in 100 g of skin-peeled apple and green grape were also found to be 0.052 mg and 0.099 mg, respectively. These values were compared with data obtained from earlier reports using the HPLC method [48,55,56], and our results indicate that the proposed assay is reliable.

Table 2 summarizes the various techniques for detecting Que, including our own suggested methodology. Our approach is uncomplicated and does not entail intricate procedures, unlike the electrochemical technique and other optical sensors. Furthermore, our sensor is capable of detecting both Que and Rut compounds.

A novel optical sensor utilizing the inner filter effect has been introduced to detect Que and Rut. To function as the sensor's signal source, a lead-free copper halide perovskite named OA3Cu2I4Cl (OACuH) was successfully synthesized using a hybrid organic–inorganic approach. OACuH is stable and well-dispersed in aqueous solutions. Upon exposure to Que or Rut, OACuH fluorescence decreased by inner filter effect. The proposed sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and a low limit of detection ranging from 0.1 to 200 µM. Reliable outcomes for the proposed assay were confirmed via testing on apple and green grape samples.

Bui The Huy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Le Thi Cam Huong: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mirkomil Sharipov: Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Hai Bang Truong: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yong-Ill Lee: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grand funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (NRF-2023R1A2C1004414).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110325.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extraction, purification, and determination by HPLC of quercetin in some italian wines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Buiarelli, et al., Extraction, Purification, and Determination by HPLC of Quercetin in Some Italian Wines, Food Analytical Methods, 11 (2018) 3558-3562. 10.1007/s12161-018-1337-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel fast identification and determination of free polyphenols in untreated craft beers by HPLC-PDA-ESI-MS/MS in SIR mode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Petrucci, et al., Novel Fast Identification and Determination of Free Polyphenols in Untreated Craft Beers by HPLC-PDA-ESI-MS/MS in SIR Mode, J. Agric. Food Chem., 68 (2020) 7984-7994. 10.1021/acs.jafc.0c02802.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validation of HPLC-UV method for simultaneous determination of quercetin and luteolin from chartamus tinctorius L in solid lipid nanoparticles incorporated in floating gel in situ formulation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.D. Permana, et al., Validation of HPLC-UV method for simultaneous determination of quercetin and luteolin from chartamus tinctorius L in solid lipid nanoparticles incorporated in floating gel in situ formulation, Microchem. J., 194 (2023) 109373. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109373.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H. Teng, L. Guo, Z. Mei, S. Chen, Study on the TLC identification and determination of the quercetin in Berchemia lineata, 2011 IEEE International Symposium on IT in Medicine and Education, 2011, pp. 39-42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of quercetin and Carbendazim in wine samples using disposable electrochemical sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.A. Raymundo-Pereira, et al., Simultaneous Detection of Quercetin and Carbendazim in Wine Samples Using Disposable Electrochemical Sensors, ChemElectroChem, 7 (2020) 3074-3081. https://doi.org/10.1002/celc.202000788.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel multicomponent TMDC, MoS2–WS2–CoSx, as an effective electrocatalyst for simultaneous detection ultra-levels of prednisolone and rutin in human body fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Z. Mohammadi, M. Dadkhodazadeh, T. Rohani, A novel multicomponent TMDC, MoS2–WS2–CoSx, as an effective electrocatalyst for simultaneous detection ultra-levels of prednisolone and rutin in human body fluids, Microchem. J., 164 (2021) 106019. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vanadium (III) -catechol functionalized copper metal–organic framework for sensitive detection of quercetin in biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. bakhshi, et al., Vanadium (III) -catechol functionalized copper metal–organic framework for sensitive detection of quercetin in biological fluids, Microchem. J., 193 (2023) 109064. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric detection of pH and quercetin based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped highly luminescent graphene-analogous flakes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Gao, et al., Fluorometric detection of pH and quercetin based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped highly luminescent graphene-analogous flakes, Analyst, 145 (2020) 115-121. 10.1039/C9AN02077B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots as a potential sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kadian, G. Manik, Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots as a potential sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin, Food Chem., 317 (2020) 126457. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126457.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective optosensing of quercetin based on novel complexation with yttrium ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.N. Nghia, B.T. Huy, Y.-I. Lee, Highly sensitive and selective optosensing of quercetin based on novel complexation with yttrium ions, Analyst, 145 (2020) 3376-3384. 10.1039/D0AN00117A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of tiny carbon dots with high quantum yield using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as support for selective “turn-off-on” detection of rutin and Al3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, et al., Synthesis of tiny carbon dots with high quantum yield using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as support for selective “turn-off-on” detection of rutin and Al3+, Carbon, 143 (2019) 391-401. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.11.049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning the aggregation/disaggregation behaviours of ZnSe quantum dots for high-sensitivity fluorescent rutin sensors
